title: Dyeing with bracken fern
subtitle: Soft yellow-green from bracken shoots and fronds on pre-mordanted wool
excerpt: Bracken (Pteridium aquilinum) gives a soft olive-yellow to yellow-green on pre-mordanted wool. Young spring shoots give a cleaner yellow; summer fronds give a more muted olive. Iron mordant shifts the result toward darker olive-brown.
sourceNotes:
- Bracken fern (Pteridium aquilinum) as a natural dye source is documented in multiple public domain and contemporary natural dye references. No specific source quotation used.
- Toxicity note for Pteridium aquilinum (ptaquiloside) is established botanical/toxicological public domain knowledge.

glossaryTerms.definition: A metallic salt used to fix dye to fibre; alum (potassium aluminium sulphate) is the most common.
The process of pre-treating fibre with a mordant before dyeing so the colour bonds permanently to the fibre.
The dry weight of the fibre to be dyed; the base measurement for calculating mordant and dyestuff quantities.
A small addition of iron mordant near the end of dyeing; darkens colour and shifts greens toward olive-brown.
The resistance of a dyed colour to fading under light exposure; rated 1 (poor) to 8 (excellent).
A naturally occurring plant compound that can assist dye take-up and mordant adhesion; bracken contains moderate levels.
A spent dye bath used for at least one batch of fibre; subsequent batches in the same bath give progressively paler shades.

content.text: Bracken fern (Pteridium aquilinum) grows across most of Britain and is easy to find in large quantities. Young spring fronds give a clean warm yellow-green; summer fronds give a deeper, more muted olive. The colour shifts with the season and the
mordant
used, making bracken a good plant for experimenters who want to learn how
mordanting
affects the final colour.
Dye pots and tools are dedicated and never used for food. Wear gloves and work in a ventilated space.

Bracken contains ptaquiloside and should never be eaten. Handling the plant for dyeing is safe with ordinary precautions: wash hands after contact with fresh fronds and avoid rubbing eyes. The prepared dye bath does not pose a contact risk.

items: Fresh bracken fronds: 200% weight of fibre by dry weight
Pre-mordanted wool: mordanted with alum at 15% WOF
Iron mordant (ferrous sulphate) for optional modifier

content.text: Method
Step 1: Pre-mordant the wool. Dissolve alum at 15%
weight of fibre
in warm water. Add the pre-wetted wool. Raise to 80°C over 30 minutes, hold for 45 minutes. Cool to 40°C, remove, and squeeze gently.

Step 2: Chop or tear the bracken fronds into pieces roughly 10 cm long to help release the dye. Place in the muslin straining bag. Cover with cold water in the dye pot. Soak for 1 hour if time allows; soaking improves extraction.
Step 3: Bring the pot to a simmer at 80°C and hold for 45 minutes. Remove and discard the bracken bag. The bath will be greenish-yellow to yellow-brown.
Step 4: Allow the dye bath to cool to 50°C. Add the pre-wetted mordanted wool. Raise to 80°C over 20 minutes.
Step 5: Hold at 80°C for 45-60 minutes, turning gently every 10 minutes. Do not boil.
Step 6: Cool to below 40°C. Remove the wool, rinse in water at the same temperature, and rinse until the water runs clear. Hang to dry away from direct sunlight.
Mordant variations
Bracken responds well to mordant variation. The
tannin
content in the plant helps colour bind even on lighter mordants:
Alum: soft yellow-green (spring fronds) to warm olive-yellow (summer fronds).
Light-fastness
is moderate. The colour holds well on decorative items and accessories but will fade on garments worn regularly in direct sun.
Iron modifier: add ferrous sulphate at 2%
weight of fibre
in the last 10 minutes of dyeing. Shifts from olive-yellow to a deeper olive-brown or khaki. The
iron modifier
also slightly improves light-fastness.
Alum on a tannin pre-bath: if a richer colour is wanted, pre-treat the wool in a weak oak gall or black tea bath (no mordant needed for the pre-bath) before
mordanting
. The tannin improves alum adhesion and deepens the resulting green.
Wastewater disposal
The spent dye bath contains low-level plant extractives and alum. Allow the bath to cool fully. An alum-only
exhaust bath

can be poured down a household drain in small quantities at home-dyeing concentrations. If an iron modifier was used, dispose of the spent bath by pouring it onto garden soil away from water courses; at these concentrations it acts as a mild soil acidifier rather than a pollutant. The spent bracken plant material goes into general waste or compost (not food compost).

items.problem: The colour is much paler than expected
items.solution: Summer fronds harvested after the bracken has fully dried or browned give very little dye. Use green fronds only. Increase the frond quantity to 300% WOF, or soak the fronds overnight before simmering to maximise extraction.

items.problem: The colour is uneven or blotchy
items.solution: The wool was not sufficiently pre-wetted, or the temperature was raised too quickly. Soak wool for at least 30 minutes in lukewarm water before adding to the dye bath. Raise the temperature no more than 5°C per 5 minutes and turn the wool gently throughout.

items.problem: The iron modifier made the wool feel rough or brittle
items.solution: Too much iron was used. Iron at above 3% WOF can damage wool over time. Reduce to 1-2% WOF on the next batch, and rinse thoroughly after using iron to remove all residue from the fibre.
